ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The work mainly adapts the existing SICK construction procedure to Polish by defining language-specific transformation rules, adding bidirectional entailment annotation, and inserting a human verification step for automatically transformed sentences. Producing a 10K Polish test corpus comparable to SICK is useful but represents an incremental, application-focused contribution rather than a fundamentally new method. The paper clearly differentiates its modifications from prior SICK work but does not introduce novel algorithms or theoretical innovations. | B(3): This work is an incremental but useful adaptation of the existing SICK corpus construction procedure to Polish: building a 10K-pair Polish evaluation set is not a fundamentally new idea but addresses an under-resourced language need. The paper contributes concrete, language-specific modifications (dependency-based transformation rules, bidirectional entailment annotation, and human verification of automatically transformed sentences) which are non-trivial extensions of prior practice. These changes clearly differentiate the contribution from prior SICK-style work and would be informative to experts working on dataset creation for morphologically rich or low-resource languages.

reviewer_a.justification: The work mainly adapts the existing SICK construction procedure to Polish by defining language-specific transformation rules, adding bidirectional entailment annotation, and inserting a human verification step for automatically transformed sentences. Producing a 10K Polish test corpus comparable to SICK is useful but represents an incremental, application-focused contribution rather than a fundamentally new method. The paper clearly differentiates its modifications from prior SICK work but does not introduce novel algorithms or theoretical innovations.

reviewer_b.justification: This work is an incremental but useful adaptation of the existing SICK corpus construction procedure to Polish: building a 10K-pair Polish evaluation set is not a fundamentally new idea but addresses an under-resourced language need. The paper contributes concrete, language-specific modifications (dependency-based transformation rules, bidirectional entailment annotation, and human verification of automatically transformed sentences) which are non-trivial extensions of prior practice. These changes clearly differentiate the contribution from prior SICK-style work and would be informative to experts working on dataset creation for morphologically rich or low-resource languages.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(1): The paper does not compare against any baselines ("Baselines compared: none mentioned"), so it provides no empirical comparisons to prior methods. While it situates itself relative to prior datasets (SICK, SemEval, ImageFlickr) and reports inter-annotator agreement for the new Polish corpus, it does not evaluate or contrast CDS models under the same data/metrics or acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably. Thus meaningful comparative evaluation with prior work is absent. | B(2): The paper does not report comparisons against any baselines (none mentioned) and evaluates only dataset construction quality via inter-annotator agreement, so it fails to compare proposed resources or methods to prior modeling baselines. Comparisons are therefore not performed under the same conditions or metrics as prior CDS work. The authors do discuss and adapt the SICK procedure and report related work coverage as adequate, and they clearly analyze the language-specific differences from SICK, but key empirical baseline comparisons are missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does not compare against any baselines ("Baselines compared: none mentioned"), so it provides no empirical comparisons to prior methods. While it situates itself relative to prior datasets (SICK, SemEval, ImageFlickr) and reports inter-annotator agreement for the new Polish corpus, it does not evaluate or contrast CDS models under the same data/metrics or acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably. Thus meaningful comparative evaluation with prior work is absent.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper does not report comparisons against any baselines (none mentioned) and evaluates only dataset construction quality via inter-annotator agreement, so it fails to compare proposed resources or methods to prior modeling baselines. Comparisons are therefore not performed under the same conditions or metrics as prior CDS work. The authors do discuss and adapt the SICK procedure and report related work coverage as adequate, and they clearly analyze the language-specific differences from SICK, but key empirical baseline comparisons are missing.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP venue because it produces a sizable, SICK-comparable Polish evaluation dataset (10K sentence pairs) addressing compositional distributional semantics for an under-resourced language, uses established annotation practices (three judges per pair) and reports standard agreement metrics (Fleiss' κ = 0.732, Krippendorff’s α = 0.78). The paper targets the right community and uses venue-expected methodology by adapting SICK construction with language-specific dependency transformation rules and human verification. A minor concern is the apparent lack of accompanying baselines, released code, or downstream evaluations to demonstrate immediate utility, which weakens but does not preclude acceptance. | B(4): The paper presents a language-specific Polish evaluation dataset for compositional distributional semantics (10K sentence pairs annotated for relatedness and entailment) and adapts the well-known SICK construction procedure with sensible modifications, which is squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue. The methodology and evaluation use community-standard resources and agreement metrics (Fleiss' κ = 0.732, Krippendorff’s α = 0.78), and the target audience (semanticists and dataset researchers) is appropriate. Minor concerns are that the contribution is primarily an adaptation of an existing English resource (language-specific rule creation and human verification) and no baselines or released code are mentioned, which may limit perceived novelty/impact at the very top tier.

reviewer_a.justification: This is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP venue because it produces a sizable, SICK-comparable Polish evaluation dataset (10K sentence pairs) addressing compositional distributional semantics for an under-resourced language, uses established annotation practices (three judges per pair) and reports standard agreement metrics (Fleiss' κ = 0.732, Krippendorff’s α = 0.78). The paper targets the right community and uses venue-expected methodology by adapting SICK construction with language-specific dependency transformation rules and human verification. A minor concern is the apparent lack of accompanying baselines, released code, or downstream evaluations to demonstrate immediate utility, which weakens but does not preclude acceptance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a language-specific Polish evaluation dataset for compositional distributional semantics (10K sentence pairs annotated for relatedness and entailment) and adapts the well-known SICK construction procedure with sensible modifications, which is squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue. The methodology and evaluation use community-standard resources and agreement metrics (Fleiss' κ = 0.732, Krippendorff’s α = 0.78), and the target audience (semanticists and dataset researchers) is appropriate. Minor concerns are that the contribution is primarily an adaptation of an existing English resource (language-specific rule creation and human verification) and no baselines or released code are mentioned, which may limit perceived novelty/impact at the very top tier.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper provides a thorough dataset-construction procedure and reports key validation metrics (10K sentence pairs; Fleiss' κ=0.732 for entailment; Krippendorff’s α=0.78 for relatedness) and explicitly discusses limitations of automatic dependency-based transformations and human verification. However, it lacks experimental comparisons or baseline evaluations of CDS models on the new dataset, and contains no ablation or sensitivity analyses of the transformation rules or annotation protocol. Related work coverage is described as adequate and limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility is only partial (language-specific rule sets and no code/hyperparameters reported). Overall, the work is adequate for releasing a dataset but misses important empirical analyses to fully support broader claims about model validation. | B(3): The paper provides a clear, methodical procedure for building a Polish CDS evaluation dataset and reports relevant annotation quality metrics (κ = 0.732, α = 0.78), which supports its central claim about dataset construction. However, it lacks experiments applying or benchmarking CDS models on the new dataset and contains no ablation or sensitivity analyses of the transformation rules or verification steps, limiting evidence for broader utility. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and several limitations (parser errors, language-specific rules, necessity of human verification) are explicitly discussed. Reproducibility is partially addressed by a detailed methodological description but no code or hyperparameters are provided and some language-specific rule definitions may be hard to replicate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a thorough dataset-construction procedure and reports key validation metrics (10K sentence pairs; Fleiss' κ=0.732 for entailment; Krippendorff’s α=0.78 for relatedness) and explicitly discusses limitations of automatic dependency-based transformations and human verification. However, it lacks experimental comparisons or baseline evaluations of CDS models on the new dataset, and contains no ablation or sensitivity analyses of the transformation rules or annotation protocol. Related work coverage is described as adequate and limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility is only partial (language-specific rule sets and no code/hyperparameters reported). Overall, the work is adequate for releasing a dataset but misses important empirical analyses to fully support broader claims about model validation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a clear, methodical procedure for building a Polish CDS evaluation dataset and reports relevant annotation quality metrics (κ = 0.732, α = 0.78), which supports its central claim about dataset construction. However, it lacks experiments applying or benchmarking CDS models on the new dataset and contains no ablation or sensitivity analyses of the transformation rules or verification steps, limiting evidence for broader utility. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and several limitations (parser errors, language-specific rules, necessity of human verification) are explicitly discussed. Reproducibility is partially addressed by a detailed methodological description but no code or hyperparameters are provided and some language-specific rule definitions may be hard to replicate.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses an important practical gap by producing a 10K Polish sentence-pair corpus for relatedness and entailment (modeled on SICK) in an under-resourced language, with reasonable inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.732, α=0.78). This resource and the described language-specific transformation/verification procedure will enable evaluation of Polish CDS models and encourage work on non-English compositional semantics, but it is primarily a dataset/method adaptation rather than a conceptual shift in the field. Its impact is therefore moderate: likely valuable and reused within the Polish and multilingual semantics communities, but not transformative beyond the subfield. | B(3): The paper fills an important gap for an under-resourced language by producing a SICK-comparable Polish corpus of 10K sentence pairs annotated for relatedness and entailment. Its language-specific adaptations (transformation rules, bidirectional entailment annotation, human verification) and reasonable inter-annotator agreement (κ = 0.732 for entailment; α = 0.78 for relatedness) make the dataset a practical resource that others are likely to use. Because it is primarily a dataset construction effort without reported model baselines or theoretical changes to CDS, its impact is useful and enabling but not transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical gap by producing a 10K Polish sentence-pair corpus for relatedness and entailment (modeled on SICK) in an under-resourced language, with reasonable inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.732, α=0.78). This resource and the described language-specific transformation/verification procedure will enable evaluation of Polish CDS models and encourage work on non-English compositional semantics, but it is primarily a dataset/method adaptation rather than a conceptual shift in the field. Its impact is therefore moderate: likely valuable and reused within the Polish and multilingual semantics communities, but not transformative beyond the subfield.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper fills an important gap for an under-resourced language by producing a SICK-comparable Polish corpus of 10K sentence pairs annotated for relatedness and entailment. Its language-specific adaptations (transformation rules, bidirectional entailment annotation, human verification) and reasonable inter-annotator agreement (κ = 0.732 for entailment; α = 0.78 for relatedness) make the dataset a practical resource that others are likely to use. Because it is primarily a dataset construction effort without reported model baselines or theoretical changes to CDS, its impact is useful and enabling but not transformative.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodological flow (selection of images, seed sentences, language-specific transformations, annotation) and states its main contributions early (procedure for building a Polish CDS evaluation set, modifications to SICK, and reported agreement scores). Writing quality is reported as good and key results are presented concretely (10K sentence pairs, κ = 0.732, α = 0.78), which supports clarity. However, the summary does not mention notation or the presence/effectiveness of figures and tables, so those presentation elements cannot be fully assessed from the provided information. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized and follows a logical flow (data selection -> seed sentences -> transformations -> pairing -> annotation), and its main contributions (Polish CDS evaluation dataset and SICK adaptations) are stated clearly early. Notation does not seem to be a barrier (the paper mainly describes procedures and annotation metrics) and the methodological steps and examples are described concretely (including reported agreement statistics κ and α). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate. Minor presentation gaps remain in the summary (e.g., no mention of figures/tables or baseline comparisons), so I deduct one point for those small omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodological flow (selection of images, seed sentences, language-specific transformations, annotation) and states its main contributions early (procedure for building a Polish CDS evaluation set, modifications to SICK, and reported agreement scores). Writing quality is reported as good and key results are presented concretely (10K sentence pairs, κ = 0.732, α = 0.78), which supports clarity. However, the summary does not mention notation or the presence/effectiveness of figures and tables, so those presentation elements cannot be fully assessed from the provided information.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized and follows a logical flow (data selection -> seed sentences -> transformations -> pairing -> annotation), and its main contributions (Polish CDS evaluation dataset and SICK adaptations) are stated clearly early. Notation does not seem to be a barrier (the paper mainly describes procedures and annotation metrics) and the methodological steps and examples are described concretely (including reported agreement statistics κ and α). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate. Minor presentation gaps remain in the summary (e.g., no mention of figures/tables or baseline comparisons), so I deduct one point for those small omissions.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims (a procedure for building a Polish CDS evaluation dataset and the creation of a 10K sentence-pair corpus) are supported by a described construction pipeline and reported inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.732 for entailment, α=0.78 for relatedness). There are no mathematical derivations to assess. The experimental methodology for dataset annotation is generally sound (three annotators, leader verification, appropriate agreement metrics), but it lacks comparative baselines or quantitative validation of the automatic transformation step beyond human verification and acknowledges dependency-parsing errors and language-specific rule dependence. Overall the conclusions are reasonable given the presented evidence, with the caveat that the procedure’s reliability depends on imperfect parsing and ad hoc transformation rules.»}్(Note: extra characters? reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s central claims (a procedure for building a Polish CDS evaluation dataset, a 10K sentence-pair corpus, and specific adaptations to the SICK construction process) are described and supported by a concrete methodology and reported inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss’ κ=0.732 for entailment; Krippendorff’s α=0.78 for relatedness). There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the dataset-construction methodology (dependency-based transformations, human verification, three annotators per pair) is appropriate, but reliance on imperfect parsing and language-specific hand-crafted rules that required post-corrections is a noted limitation. The work would be stronger with empirical validation that the dataset is comparable to SICK for CDS model evaluation (e.g., baselines or downstream experiments) and with code/reproducibility details, so I mark only minor technical issues.

main_claims: Presents a procedure for building a Polish evaluation dataset for validation of compositional distributional semantics (CDS) models.
Builds a Polish test corpus of 10K sentence pairs annotated for semantic relatedness and entailment, intended to be comparable to the English SICK corpus.

Introduces modifications to the SICK construction process for Polish, including language-specific transformation rules, bidirectional entailment annotation, and human verification of automatically transformed sentences.
Reports inter-annotator agreement for the resulting dataset (κ = 0.732 for entailment using Fleiss' kappa; α = 0.78 for relatedness using Krippendorff’s alpha).
_summary.methodology: Adapt the SICK corpus construction steps to Polish by selecting images from the 8K ImageFlickr dataset, writing Polish seed sentences, performing automatic language-specific transformations on dependency structures, arranging original and transformed sentences into ordered pairs with explicit pairing rules, and obtaining human annotations (three judges per ordered pair with a leader verifying transformed sentences and post-corrections).
datasets: SemEval-2012 Semantic Textual Similarity dataset (Agirre et al., 2012)
New Polish evaluation dataset (10K sentence pairs, to be made publicly available)
metrics: Fleiss' kappa (κ) for inter-annotator agreement on entailment
Krippendorff's alpha (α) for inter-annotator agreement on relatedness
Relatedness score computed as an average of Likert-scale ratings (5-point scale mentioned for SICK)
limitations: Polish is under-resourced so necessary extraneous resources for the original SICK procedure are lacking.
Automatic transformation process is based on dependency parsing which is imperfect and can produce erroneous transformed sentences requiring human verification.
Transformation rules are language-specific and had to be defined from scratch for Polish, so the SICK procedure cannot be adapted without modifications.
Some sentences accepted by some annotators were unacceptable to others, necessitating post-corrections and re-annotation.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - compositional distributional semantics; dataset construction for semantic relatedness and entailment
